text: This video shows a robotic Fruchmann-Goldberg procedure for complete rectal prolapse.
The patient is a forty-nine-year-old woman with a history of chronic constipation and a previous hemorrhoidectomy.
She had complained of rectal prolapse associated with incomplete bowel evacuation, mucus discharge, pain, and bleeding.
A seven centimeters complete rectal prolapse was diagnosed.
At the preoperative workup, the patient was found to have a redundant sigmoid colon and normal sphincter function.
Considering the preoperative findings and that the patient was otherwise healthy, we decided to perform a posterior suture rectopoxy with sigmoid resection.
This procedure was performed using the Da Vinci XI platform.
The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg position with a right tilt, allowing for a thorough exploration of the operative field.
The surgical view displays the aortic bifurcation, common iliac arteries, and the inferior mesenteric artery, highlighting these major vascular structures in the operative field.
The surgeon secures the uterus to the anterior abdominal wall with a monofilament suture, enhancing exposure of the pelvic space for the procedure.
The surgeon observes a deep pouch of Douglas and confirms the redundancy of the sigmoid colon, which is consistent with preoperative colonoscopy findings.
The surgeon retracts the colon from the pelvis and suspends the recto-sigmoid mesentery using a tip-up fenestrated grasper, providing clear visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection over the sacral promontory, making a perineal incision on the right side of the mesentery, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon mobilizes the mesorectum posteriorly along the rectal proper fascia, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection using a monopolar hook, minimizing bleeding and enhancing visibility of the anatomical plane within the pelvic space.
The surgeon performs posterior mobilization of the mesorectum down to the pelvic floor, continuing the dissection along the rectal proper fascia.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the right lateral attachments, dividing the right lateral stalk, with visible separation of tissue layers.

The surgeon uses stable tissue traction and counter traction, aided by the magnified three-dimensional view from the robotic platform, to carefully preserve the pelvic autonomic nerves during dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection close to the rectal proper fascia, carefully avoiding injury to the hypogastric nerve.
The surgeon performs mesorectal dissection on the left side, carefully separating the mesorectal tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision and continues the division of lateral attachments, extending the dissection down to the level previously reached in the pelvic region.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the rectum, dividing the lateral stalks, which are visible as fibrous tissue structures being separated from the rectum.
Following the complete mobilization of the rectum, the surgeon proceeds with cranial dissection, advancing upwards from the previously dissected area.
The surgeon retracts the mesozygnoid medially and divides its V-shaped parietal attachment, creating an opening in the mesentery.
The surgeon continues lateral detachment along the white line of Toldt, moving cephalad towards the descending colon, while preserving the splenic flexure.
The surgeon retracts the colon by grasping the epiphylic appendices, carefully minimizing the risk of bowel injury during the procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the opening in the mesentery of the sigmoid colon, providing visual access to the intersigmoid recess.
The superior rectal artery is visually identified as it courses toward the posterior aspect of the rectum.
The surgeon clears mesenteric fat from the wall of the upper rectum at the level of the planned transection, exposing the rectal tissue for further surgical steps.
The blood supply provided by the superior rectal artery is preserved to minimize the risk of anastomotic leakage.
The surgeon completes the preparation of the rectal wall using a bipolar electrosurgical dissector, ensuring precise dissection and hemostasis of the tissue.
The surgeon transects the rectum using a 45mm robotic linear stapler, applying two blue reloads to complete the division.
The technical features of the robotic stapler enable improved staple line formation.
Following the transection of the rectum, the surgeon divides the mesazygmoid, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon suspends the sigmoid colon by grasping its proximal and distal ends, while dividing the mesentery using a bipolar electrosurgical dissector.
The surgeon continues the division from the level of rectal transection up to the opening in the mesazygmoid, carefully dissecting close to the bowel wall to preserve the superior rectal vessels.
The surgeon identifies the level for the proximal bowel section, ensuring there is no tension on the anastomosis, and excises the redundant sigmoid colon.
A surgical clip is placed to mark the level of section on the bowel, indicating the precise location for division.
The surgeon extracts the colon through a suprapubic incision and performs proximal transection using a purse string device, completing the sigmoidectomy.

The surgeon inserts the anvil of a 29mm circular powered stapler into the lumen of the proximal colon. Bowel stump perfusion is assessed using endocyanin green fluorescence imaging, which highlights the vascular supply to the tissue.
The surgeon extends the stapler trocar, piercing the anterior rectal wall, preparing for the anastomosis.
The surgeon couples the anvil to the circular stapler and checks the orientation of the mesentery to ensure proper alignment for the anastomosis.
The surgeon inspects the bowel stumps, ensuring that no extraneous tissue is included in the surgical field.
The surgeon fires the stapler, creating an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis, visually joining the bowel ends with a circular staple line.
The surgeon performs a posterior suture rectopexy, securing the rectum in place with sutures.
The surgeon stretches the right peritoneal flap toward the sacral promontory and sutures it in place, securing the rectum in an elevated position.
Two interrupted 2-0 polyester stitches are placed through the peritoneal flap and the prosacral fascia, securing the flap in position.
The surgeon uses endo-wrist technology to facilitate suturing and tying, enhancing precision and control during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the prosacral veins, pelvic nerves, and retroperitoneal structures to prevent any unintentional injury during the procedure.
Suture rectopoxy provides good results in terms of recurrence and avoiding mesh-related complications.
The surgeon uses a running suture to approximate the incised peritoneal surfaces on the right side, ensuring the tissue edges are closely aligned.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 barbed, slowly absorbable suture to secure the peritoneal flap to the prosacral fascia.
The surgeon performs closure in an oblique direction, securing the rectum to promote posterior fibrosis, using sutures to fixate the tissue.
The surgeon performs a running suture to approximate the incised peritoneal surfaces on the left side, mirroring the technique used on the right side.
The rectum is visually observed to be straight and firm in its position following the suturing process.

Following the completion of rectopexy, the surgeon places several interrupted absorbable stitches to reinforce the anastomotic suture line, aiming to prevent tension that could result from postoperative straining.
The surgeon performs a leak test by insufflating air and injecting methylene blue dye to check for any leaks in the anastomosis.
The surgeon places a surgical drain near the anastomosis site to facilitate fluid drainage, while ensuring hemostasis by checking for any bleeding in the surrounding tissue.
